0000_apple_black_rot.jpg.label: AppleBlackRot
0000_apple_black_rot.jpg.caption: Apple black rot typically appears as circular, sunken, black lesions on the fruit, surrounded by a velvety greenish-black spore mass.
0000_apple_black_rot.jpg.management: Remove all infected leaves, fruit, and mummified fruit from tree and destroy by burning to prevent ascospore production. Prune infected young shoots with cracked, coarse bark during winter. Practice good sanitation by removing all crop debris after harvest. Apply protective fungicides from silver tip to harvest, particularly copper-based fungicides or Bordeaux mixture when wet periods are forecast. Fungal fruiting bodies appear as dots arranged in circular patterns on fruit. The disease overwinters in twigs and fruit remaining on trees, spreading during rainfall.

0003_apple_mosaic_virus.jpg.label: AppleMosaicVirus

0003_apple_mosaic_virus.jpg.management: Prevention is essential as there is no cure. Use only certified virus-free rootstock and scion wood for all propagation. 0006_apple_rust.jpg.label: AppleRust
0006_apple_rust.jpg.caption: Apple rust appears as bright orange or yellowish-orange spots on the leaves and fruit of infected trees.
0006_apple_rust.jpg.management: Cedar apple rust requires two hosts: apple and Eastern red cedar (Juniperus virginiana). Plant resistant apple varieties (such as Dayton and Delicious) where possible. Remove nearby Eastern red cedar trees within 1/4 mile if feasible. If growing susceptible varieties in proximity to cedar, follow a protective fungicide program. Spores from cedar galls are released in spring after warm rains and can travel 2-3 miles by wind. Apply fungicides preventatively during the infection period in spring. The fungus forms galls on cedar, producing spores that infect apple leaves and fruit, causing bright orange or yellow patches surrounded by red bands.

0009_apple_scab.jpg.label: AppleScab
0009_apple_scab.jpg.caption: Apple scab is characterized by raised, dark-colored lesions on the fruit's surface, resembling scabs.
0009_apple_scab.jpg.management: Apple scab (Venturia inaequalis) is a major fungal disease. Collect and burn fallen leaves and twigs to prevent ascospore production. Prune infected young shoots with cracked bark during winter. Apply urea (2 kg/acre) at pre-leaf fall and dolomitic lime (2.5 tons/acre) in autumn on fallen leaves to accelerate decomposition. Apply systemic fungicides like triazoles (e.g., Systhane) which are effective as preventive, curative, and eradicant. Captan (contact fungicide) is effective and not prone to resistance development. Apply fungicides from silver tip stage through growing season. The disease causes dark, scabby marks on fruit and leaves with olive-green velvety lesions. Fungus overwinters on plant debris.

0015_basil_downy_mildew.jpg.management: Grow tolerant or resistant varieties (lemon and spice basils show resistance). Apply protective fungicides preventatively. Ensure good air circulation around plants especially in greenhouse settings. Use drip irrigation to avoid wetting foliage - water at the base of plants instead of overhead. Symptoms include yellowing leaves with discoloration often beginning around middle vein, gray fuzzy or downy growth on lower leaf surfaces, and brown to black angular necrotic patches. The disease can spread by contaminated seed. Remove infected plants promptly. Avoid working in wet conditions that favor spore spread.

0018_bean_halo_blight.jpg.label: BeanHaloBlight
0018_bean_halo_blight.jpg.caption: Bean halo blight appears as small, yellowish halos surrounding dark-brown spots on the leaves and pods.
0018_bean_halo_blight.jpg.management: Plant only certified disease-free seed. Rotate crops regularly with non-host plants. Remove all crop debris from field after harvest. Avoid working in fields when foliage is wet to prevent bacterial spread. The bacterium (Pseudomonas syringae pv. phaseolicola) overwinters in crop residue and is more severe when foliage remains wet for extended periods. Symptoms include small angular water-soaked spots on leaf undersides that turn necrotic, with characteristic yellow halos surrounding lesions. Lesions on expanding leaves may cause distortion. Red-brown lesions may appear on pods, sometimes oozing or turning tan. Copper-based bactericides may provide some control when applied preventatively.

0021_bean_mosaic_virus.jpg.label: BeanMosaicVirus
0021_bean_mosaic_virus.jpg.caption: Bean mosaic virus causes mottled and distorted leaves in bean plants with distinct mosaic-like patterns.
0021_bean_mosaic_virus.jpg.management: No cure exists for viral infections. Prevention is critical: use only certified virus-free seed as Bean Common Mosaic Virus (BCMV) is seed-transmitted in Phaseolus species. Remove and bag infected plants immediately to prevent aphid transmission to healthy plants. The virus spreads among plants by aphids in a non-persistent manner (aphids acquire virus by tasting infected plants and transmit to next plant they taste). Insecticides are ineffective because transmission occurs before aphids can be killed. Plant resistant varieties where available. Control weed hosts near bean plantings. Symptoms include mottled dark and light green patterns on leaves, leaf distortion, yellow dots, stunted growth, and black root necrosis.

0024_bean_rust.jpg.label: BeanRust

0024_bean_rust.jpg.management: Grow resistant varieties where available. Remove and destroy infected crop debris after harvest. Follow crop rotation with non-host plants. Keep fields weed-free. Apply suitable fungicides if disease becomes severe. The fungus (Uromyces appendiculatus) produces raised brick-red rust pustules (uredinia) surrounded by yellow halos, initially appearing as small yellow/white spots. Premature leaf drop may occur with severe infections. Urediniospores spread between fields by air. Black teliospores formed at end of season overwinter in field debris and serve as inoculum for next season. Favor resistant varieties as primary control method.

0027_bell_pepper_leaf.jpg.label: BellPepperLeaf

0027_bell_pepper_leaf.jpg.management: Grow in well-drained, fertile soil with pH 6.0-6.8 in full sun. Maintain consistent soil moisture through regular watering, especially during fruit development. Apply mulch to conserve moisture and suppress weeds. Fertilize with balanced nutrients, particularly calcium to prevent blossom end rot. Provide support for plants as needed. Practice crop rotation. Monitor for pests including aphids, whiteflies, and thrips. Ensure good air circulation between plants. Peppers are warm-season crops requiring temperatures above 15°C (60°F) for optimal growth.

0042_carrot_cavity_spot.jpg.management: Cavity spot is caused by the soil-borne oomycete Pythium spp. and appears as small sunken lesions on carrot roots that enlarge into cavities. 0054_cherry_leaf_spot.jpg.label: CherryLeafSpot
0054_cherry_leaf_spot.jpg.caption: Cherry leaf spot appears as small, circular and dark spots on the leaves of cherry trees.
0054_cherry_leaf_spot.jpg.management: Caused by fungus Blumeriella jaapii (formerly Coccomyces hiemalis). Remove and destroy fallen leaves to reduce overwintering inoculum. Apply protective fungicides beginning at petal fall and continuing at regular intervals through harvest. Fungicides should include materials effective against cherry leaf spot. Prune to improve air circulation and light penetration. The disease causes purple spots on leaves that develop into small holes ('shot-hole' appearance), leading to premature defoliation. Severe defoliation weakens trees and reduces fruit production. Resistant varieties are available.

0060_citrus_canker.jpg.label: CitrusCanker
0060_citrus_canker.jpg.caption: Citrus canker appears as raised, corky lesions on citrus fruit, leaves, and stems.
0060_citrus_canker.jpg.management: Citrus canker (Xanthomonas citri) is a highly contagious bacterial disease. Use disease-free nursery stock. Remove and destroy infected trees and surrounding trees within quarantine radius. Apply preventive copper sprays during periods of new growth, especially during rainy season. Windbreaks can reduce wind-driven rain spread. The disease causes raised corky lesions on fruit, leaves, and stems. It spreads by wind-driven rain, contaminated equipment, and human activity. Decontaminate all tools and equipment. Follow quarantine regulations. There is no cure - management focuses on prevention and eradication. Plant resistant citrus varieties where available.

0063_citrus_greening_disease.jpg.label: CitrusGreeningDisease
0063_citrus_greening_disease.jpg.caption: Citrus greening disease is characterized by blotchy mottling and yellowing of leaves, stunted growth, and misshapen fruit.
0063_citrus_greening_disease.jpg.management: Citrus greening (Huanglongbing/HLB) is caused by bacteria Candidatus Liberibacter spp. and transmitted by Asian citrus psyllid. There is no cure. Management strategy includes: 1) Use disease-free nursery stock from certified sources. 2) Control psyllid vector with systemic insecticides (multiple applications per year). 3) Remove infected trees immediately to reduce inoculum. 4) Scout frequently for symptoms and psyllids. 5) Apply nutritional programs to maintain tree health. 6) Plant under protective screens in nurseries. Symptoms include blotchy mottling on leaves, yellow shoots, lopsided bitter fruit, and eventual tree death. Plant more tolerant varieties (e.g., grapefruit more tolerant than sweet orange). Area-wide management programs are most effective.

0066_coffee_leaf_rust.jpg.label: CoffeeLeafRust
0066_coffee_leaf_rust.jpg.caption: Coffee leaf rust appears as yellow-orange powdery spots on the undersides of coffee plant leaves.
0066_coffee_leaf_rust.jpg.management: Coffee leaf rust (Hemileia vastatrix) is one of the most devastating coffee diseases. Apply protective copper-based fungicides before rainy season and throughout wet periods. Plant resistant varieties (e.g., Arabica varieties with resistance genes). Remove infected leaves and fallen debris. Improve air circulation through proper spacing and pruning. The disease appears as orange-yellow powdery pustules on leaf undersides, causing premature defoliation. Optimal conditions: 21-25°C with high humidity and leaf wetness. Maintain tree vigor through proper nutrition and shade management. Biological control agents (Bacillus, Pseudomonas, white halo fungus) show promise but are not yet commercially viable.

0069_corn_gray_leaf_spot.jpg.label: CornGrayLeafSpot
0069_corn_gray_leaf_spot.jpg.caption: Corn gray leaf spot appears as small gray to tan lesions with dark borders on the leaves.
0069_corn_gray_leaf_spot.jpg.management: Gray leaf spot (Cercospora zeae-maydis) causes rectangular lesions 1-6 cm long with sharp, parallel edges that turn gray with maturity. Plant resistant hybrids - this is the most effective control. Practice crop rotation with non-corn crops. Use minimum tillage carefully as disease favors continuous corn with minimum tillage. Remove crop debris or plow under deeply. Apply foliar fungicides when necessary in high-pressure situations. The disease emerges in humid, foggy conditions with prolonged wetness. Lesions are opaque and have parallel edges following leaf veins. Can cause rapid leaf blighting and yield loss.

0072_corn_northern_leaf_blight.jpg.label: CornNorthernLeafBlight
0072_corn_northern_leaf_blight.jpg.caption: Corn northern leaf blight appears as elongated, cigar-shaped lesions with tan centers and dark borders on corn leaves.
0072_corn_northern_leaf_blight.jpg.management: Northern leaf blight (Exserohilum turcicum) produces cigar-shaped gray-green to tan lesions 2.5-15 cm long. Apply fungicides when disease appears on lower leaves before tasseling. The disease is favored by moderate temperatures (18-27°C) and high humidity. Lesions may coalesce causing extensive blighting. Most important in continuous corn production areas.

0075_corn_rust.jpg.label: CornRust
0075_corn_rust.jpg.caption: Corn rust appears as orange or reddish-brown pustules on the leaves, stems, and husks of infected plants.
0075_corn_rust.jpg.management: Common rust produces small, circular to elongate brown pustules on both leaf surfaces. Southern rust has orange pustules mainly on upper surface. Plant resistant hybrids - most effective method. Apply fungicides when disease appears on lower leaves and environmental conditions favor disease development (moderate temperatures 16-23°C with high humidity for common rust; warmer for southern rust). Scout fields regularly. The disease can develop rapidly under favorable conditions. Pustules contain masses of spores that spread by wind. Usually not economically important until after tasseling.

0078_corn_smut.jpg.label: CornSmut
0078_corn_smut.jpg.caption: Corn smut appears as large, gray-black, distorted galls or tumors on the ears, leaves, and stalks of corn plants.
0078_corn_smut.jpg.management: Common smut (Ustilago maydis) causes tumor-like galls on any above-ground plant tissue - ears, tassels, stalks, leaves. Galls are initially silvery-white, turning dark brown/black as spores mature. Plant resistant hybrids. Remove galls before they mature and release spores. Deep plow crop residue. Avoid mechanical injury to plants. Practice crop rotation. Reduce nitrogen fertilization if smut is a problem. The fungus overwinters as spores in soil and infects through wounds. Young, actively growing tissue is most susceptible. In Mexico, immature galls (huitlacoche) are considered a delicacy.

Use disease-free seed and transplants. Practice crop rotation with non-cucurbit crops (2-3 years). Remove infected plant debris. Avoid overhead irrigation. Apply copper-based bactericides preventatively. The bacterium overwinters on crop debris and spreads by water splash and contaminated tools. Symptoms include angular spots on leaves that may dry and drop out, and white bacterial ooze on lesions. Fruit lesions appear water-soaked and may crack.

0084_cucumber_bacterial_wilt.jpg.label: CucumberBacterialWilt

0084_cucumber_bacterial_wilt.jpg.management: Bacterial wilt (Erwinia tracheiphila) is transmitted by cucumber beetles. Control cucumber beetles early and throughout season using row covers, insecticides, or beetle traps. Plant resistant varieties where available. Remove and destroy infected plants immediately to prevent beetle spread. The disease causes sudden wilting of vines with bacterial ooze visible when stems are cut and pulled apart (bacterial streaming test). There is no chemical control once plants are infected. Prevention through beetle control is essential. Practice crop rotation. Remove wild cucurbit hosts near fields.

0105_grape_black_rot.jpg.label: GrapeBlackRot

0105_grape_black_rot.jpg.management: Black rot (Guignardia bidwellii) is a major disease of grapes causing fruit mummification. Remove and destroy all mummified fruit and infected canes during dormant pruning. Apply protective fungicides beginning at bud break and continuing through fruit set, with special attention during bloom. Prune vines to improve air circulation and light penetration. The fungus overwinters in mummified fruit and infected canes. Symptoms include circular reddish-brown leaf spots and rotted berries. Sanitation is critical - remove all infected material from vineyard. Plant in locations with good air drainage.

0114_grapevine_leafroll_disease.jpg.management: Leafroll disease is caused by viruses (Grapevine leafroll-associated viruses) transmitted by mealybugs and scale insects. Symptoms include downward rolling of leaf margins, reddish discoloration (red varieties) or yellowing (white varieties) between veins, delayed fruit ripening, and reduced sugar content. The disease reduces yield and fruit quality significantly.

Eliminate weed hosts (especially sowthistle) near fields. The virus causes mottling, leaf distortion, stunting, and yellowing. There is no cure. Reflective mulches may deter aphid landing. Plant resistant varieties where available. Avoid planting near older lettuce fields that may harbor disease.

0123_maple_tar_spot.jpg.label: MapleTarSpot
0123_maple_tar_spot.jpg.caption: Maple tar spot appears as black, tar-like raised spots on the leaves of maple trees.
0123_maple_tar_spot.jpg.management: Tar spot (Rhytisma acerinum and related species) causes raised black spots on maple leaves. The disease is primarily cosmetic and rarely requires treatment. Rake and remove fallen leaves to reduce overwintering spores. Ensure good air circulation around trees. Maintain tree vigor through proper watering and fertilization. Fungicides are generally not recommended as the disease causes little harm. The fungus overwinters on fallen leaves and produces spores that infect new leaves in spring. Symptoms appear as yellow spots in early summer that develop into characteristic black tar-like spots by late summer.

0126_peach_leaf_curl.jpg.label: PeachLeafCurl
0126_peach_leaf_curl.jpg.caption: Peach leaf curl causes red, curled, and distorted leaves with a blister-like appearance on peach trees.
0126_peach_leaf_curl.jpg.management: Peach leaf curl (Taphrina deformans) causes puckered, distorted leaves that become reddish and eventually drop. Apply protective fungicides (copper-based or synthetic) during dormancy before bud swell in late winter/early spring. One well-timed application provides season-long control. A second application may be needed in high rainfall areas just before bud break. The fungus overwinters on tree bark and bud scales. Prune to improve air circulation. Remove and destroy infected leaves. There are no effective treatments once infection occurs. Plant resistant varieties where available. Disease is most severe in cool, wet springs.

0129_plum_pocket_disease.jpg.management: Plum pocket (Taphrina pruni) causes fruit to become swollen, distorted, and bladder-like without developing seeds. Apply fungicides (copper or lime sulfur) during dormancy and again at bud swell before bloom. Prune to improve air circulation and remove diseased wood. Remove and destroy infected fruit before spores are released. The fungus overwinters in bark and bud scales. Disease is favored by cool, wet weather during bloom. Sanitation is important - remove mummified fruit. Plant resistant varieties if available. American plums are more susceptible than European plums.

0132_potato_early_blight.jpg.label: PotatoEarlyBlight

0132_potato_early_blight.jpg.management: Early blight (Alternaria solani) causes dark brown spots with concentric rings (target pattern) on older leaves. Use certified disease-free seed potatoes. Practice crop rotation (3-4 years) with non-solanaceous crops. Apply protective fungicides when disease appears or preventatively if conditions favor disease (warm, humid weather with leaf wetness). Remove and destroy crop debris. Ensure adequate but not excessive nitrogen. Maintain plant vigor through proper fertility and water. The fungus overwinters on crop debris and infected tubers. Symptoms appear first on lower, older leaves and progress upward.

0135_potato_late_blight.jpg.label: PotatoLateBlight
0135_potato_late_blight.jpg.caption: Potato late blight appears as dark, water-soaked lesions on leaves, stems, and tubers.
0135_potato_late_blight.jpg.management: Late blight (Phytophthora infestans) is the most destructive potato disease. Use certified disease-free seed potatoes. Plant resistant varieties where available. Apply protective fungicides preventatively starting when conditions favor disease (moderate temperatures with high humidity and leaf wetness). Scout fields regularly using prediction models (e.g., Blightcast). Remove volunteer potatoes and nightshade weeds. Hill soil over tubers to prevent spore wash-down. Destroy infected plants and cull piles. The pathogen produces water-soaked lesions on leaves with white fuzzy growth on undersides during wet weather, causing rapid plant death. Tuber rot occurs if spores wash into soil.

0138_rice_blast.jpg.label: RiceBlast
0138_rice_blast.jpg.caption: Rice blast appears as small, oval lesions with gray centers and brown borders on rice leaves.
0138_rice_blast.jpg.management: Rice blast (Magnaporthe oryzae) is one of the most important rice diseases worldwide. Plant resistant varieties - most effective control. Avoid over-fertilization with nitrogen which increases susceptibility. Ensure good water management to prevent drought stress. Apply systemic fungicides where available and economically feasible. The disease causes diamond-shaped lesions on leaves with gray centers and brown margins. It can also infect nodes (neck blast) and panicles, causing severe yield loss. The fungus produces spores that spread by wind. Disease favored by high humidity, moderate temperatures, and nitrogen excess.

0141_rice_sheath_blight.jpg.management: Sheath blight (Rhizoctonia solani) appears as oblong or elliptical lesions on leaf sheaths with pale centers and purple-brown margins. Avoid over-fertilizing with nitrogen as excessive nitrogen increases susceptibility. Time fungicide applications so first application is made at maximum tillering and second at early heading stages. Ensure proper water management. The disease occurs in all rice-growing areas and is second in importance only to blast. It spreads rapidly via irrigation water and lesions can cover entire sheaths and stems, leading to poorly filled grains. Most damaging in intensive rice production systems.

Can cause severe defoliation leading to sunscald of fruit and reduced yields.

0153_tobacco_mosaic_virus.jpg.management: Tobacco mosaic virus (TMV) is extremely stable and infectious. Use resistant varieties where available. Practice crop rotation (3-4 years). Stake and prune plants to improve air circulation. Mulch to prevent soil splash. Remove lower infected leaves. Apply protective fungicides when disease appears or conditions favor development (warm, humid weather). The fungus overwinters on crop debris. Water at base of plants. Maintain plant vigor through proper fertility. Remove crop debris after harvest. Some varieties show tolerance.

0162_tomato_late_blight.jpg.label: TomatoLateBlight
0162_tomato_late_blight.jpg.caption: Tomato late blight appears as dark, water-soaked lesions on leaves, stems, and fruits, often covered with a fuzzy, white mold.
0162_tomato_late_blight.jpg.management: Late blight (Phytophthora infestans) is the most destructive tomato disease. Plant resistant varieties (Mountain Magic, Plum Regal, Defiant). Use disease-free transplants. Apply protective fungicides (copper or synthetic) preventatively when conditions favor disease (cool, wet weather). Remove infected plants immediately. Prune to improve air circulation and remove lower leaves. Avoid overhead watering. The pathogen produces water-soaked lesions on leaves and stems with white fuzzy growth on undersides during wet weather. Can cause total crop loss. Scout regularly and destroy infected fruit and plants.

0165_tomato_leaf_mold.jpg.management: Leaf mold (Passalora fulva, formerly Cladosporium fulvum) causes yellow spots on upper leaf surface with olive-green to gray-brown fuzzy growth on undersides. Apply fungicides if necessary. Remove infected leaves. The disease is favored by high humidity (>85%) and moderate temperatures. Resistant varieties are available. Prune to improve air movement through canopy.

0168_tomato_mosaic_virus.jpg.label: TomatoMosaicVirus
0168_tomato_mosaic_virus.jpg.caption: Tomato mosaic virus causes distinct mottled patterns of light and dark green on tomato leaves.
0168_tomato_mosaic_virus.jpg.management: Tomato mosaic virus (ToMV) and tobacco mosaic virus cause mottling, distortion, and stunting. Use resistant varieties. Buy certified virus-free transplants. Disinfect tools and hands with 10% bleach or milk solution. The virus is mechanically transmitted and very stable. Avoid tobacco use near plants. Remove infected plants immediately. Control aphids. There is no cure. Symptoms include light and dark green mottling, leaf distortion, and internal browning of fruit. ToMV is seed-borne so use clean seed sources. Virus remains infectious in dried plant debris.

0174_tomato_yellow_leaf_curl_virus.jpg.label: TomatoYellowLeafCurlVirus
0174_tomato_yellow_leaf_curl_virus.jpg.caption: Tomato yellow leaf curl virus causes yellowing and curling of tomato leaves with stunted plant growth.
0174_tomato_yellow_leaf_curl_virus.jpg.management: Tomato yellow leaf curl virus (TYLCV) is transmitted by whiteflies (Bemisia tabaci). Control whiteflies through monitoring and insecticides (particularly systemic products). Use reflective mulches to deter whitefly landing. Remove infected plants immediately. Plant resistant varieties - many TYLCV-resistant varieties available. Use physical barriers (insect screening) in greenhouses. Eliminate weed hosts around fields. The virus causes upward leaf curling, yellowing, stunting, and greatly reduced fruit set. Vector control is essential as there is no cure. Scout regularly for whiteflies and TYLCV symptoms.

Symptoms include yellow mosaic patterns, distortion, and stunting of leaves and fruit. Severely infected plants may die. Fruit is often unmarketable.

0180_apple_leaf.jpg.label: AppleLeaf
0180_apple_leaf.jpg.caption: Apple leaf appearance is typically green, oval-shaped, serrated edges, and may turn yellow or brown with disease.
0180_apple_leaf.jpg.management: Maintain fertile, well-drained, loamy-sandy soil with pH 5.5-6.5. Test soil and amend with organic matter, lime, or sulfur as needed. Provide adequate moisture especially during flowering and fruiting. In hotter regions, implement regular irrigation. For tropical cultivation, practice careful management including bending shoots, tip pruning, and defoliation. Apply balanced fertilization and monitor for nutrient deficiencies (particularly iron, which causes yellowing of new growth). Prune to maintain good air circulation and tree structure. Established trees may not require fertilization if growing vigorously.

0183_banana_leaf.jpg.label: BananaLeaf
0183_banana_leaf.jpg.caption: The banana leaf is large, vibrant green, and elongated with distinct veins running through it.
0183_banana_leaf.jpg.management: Ensure well-drained soil and consistent moisture without waterlogging. Bananas require regular watering, especially during dry periods. Apply mulch around base to conserve moisture and suppress weeds. Fertilize regularly with balanced nutrients including nitrogen, phosphorus, and potassium. Remove dead leaves and pseudostems promptly. Prune suckers to maintain 2-3 stems per plant for optimal production. Monitor for pests and diseases regularly. Ensure good air circulation between plants. In commercial production, practice debudding (removing male buds) to prevent disease spread and improve fruit quality.

0186_basil_leaf.jpg.label: BasilLeaf
0186_basil_leaf.jpg.caption: Healthy basil leaf is a vibrant green color, smooth in texture, and exhibits a crisp appearance.
0186_basil_leaf.jpg.management: Basil thrives in warm conditions with daytime temperatures consistently above 21°C (70°F) and nighttime temperatures above 15.5°C (60°F). Plant in fertile, well-draining soil with pH 6.0-7.5 in full sunlight. Water regularly at base of plant, more frequently for outdoor crops. Avoid wetting leaves to prevent downy mildew. Practice careful manual weeding. Harvest regularly to encourage bushy growth. Pinch off flower buds to maintain leaf production. Basil is an annual herb that may survive multiple seasons with proper care, reaching 30-130 cm height depending on variety.

0192_blueberry_leaf.jpg.label: BlueberryLeaf

Cross-pollination improves fruit set - plant multiple varieties. Monitor for mummy berry disease and remove infected fruit.

Harvest when heads are compact and before curds begin to separate.

Apply nitrogen fertilizer: side-dress when plants are 30-45 cm tall. Plant in blocks rather than single rows for better pollination. Control weeds especially during first 6 weeks. Monitor for corn earworm, European corn borer, and fall armyworm. Practice crop rotation to prevent disease and pest buildup.

Fertilize regularly with balanced nutrients emphasizing potassium for rhizome development. Protect from strong winds. Monitor for rhizome rot, bacterial wilt, and leaf spot. 0240_plum_leaf.jpg.management: Plum trees require full sun, well-drained soil with pH 6.0-7.0, and regular care. Prune annually during dormancy to maintain vase or open-center shape for air circulation. Thin fruit when thumbnail-sized to improve size and prevent branch breakage. Apply balanced fertilization in spring. Ensure adequate but not excessive water - overwatering increases disease. Monitor for brown rot, black knot, and bacterial spot. Many plum varieties are self-sterile and require cross-pollination. Control plum curculio and aphids. Harvest when fruit yields slightly to pressure and develops characteristic color.

0243_potato_leaf.jpg.label: PotatoLeaf
0243_potato_leaf.jpg.caption: Yellowing and wilting of leaves with brown spots and necrotic lesions, often starting from the edges.
0243_potato_leaf.jpg.management: Potatoes require cool growing conditions (15-20°C / 60-68°F), full sun, and well-drained, fertile soil with pH 5.0-6.5. Plant certified disease-free seed potatoes 10-15 cm deep. Hill soil around plants as they grow to prevent greening of tubers. Ensure consistent moisture (2.5-5 cm per week) especially during tuber formation. Apply balanced fertilization emphasizing potassium. Monitor for Colorado potato beetle, aphids, and diseases. Practice crop rotation (3-4 years minimum). Harvest when vines die back naturally or 2 weeks after killing vines. Cure tubers before storage.

0246_raspberry_leaf.jpg.label: RaspberryLeaf
0246_raspberry_leaf.jpg.caption: Raspberry leaf appears with yellow or brown spots, curling, wilting, and eventually dropping.
0246_raspberry_leaf.jpg.management: Raspberries require full sun, well-drained soil with pH 5.5-6.5, and consistent moisture. Plant in raised beds in heavy soils. Provide support with trellis or posts and wire. Prune summer-bearing types by removing fruited canes after harvest; prune fall-bearing types to ground in late winter. Apply balanced fertilization in spring. Mulch to conserve moisture and suppress weeds. Ensure good air circulation through proper spacing and pruning. Monitor for anthracnose, spur blight, cane borers, and raspberry fruitworms. Thin canes to 6-8 per linear foot for best production.

0249_rice_leaf.jpg.label: RiceLeaf
0249_rice_leaf.jpg.caption: Healthy rice leaves are typically long, slender, and vibrant green in color with no visible lesions.
0249_rice_leaf.jpg.management: Rice requires warm temperatures (20-37°C / 68-99°F) and abundant water or high rainfall (800-2000mm). Plant in fields that can be flooded or have adequate irrigation. For lowland rice, maintain standing water at proper depth. Ensure adequate nitrogen, phosphorus, and potassium fertilization. Control weeds which compete for nutrients and harbor pests. Monitor for blast, bacterial blight, and sheath blight diseases. Control stem borers, plant hoppers, and other insects. Drain fields before harvest when grain is mature. Most rice is grown at 0-2500m elevation in humid lowlands and deltas.

0252_soybean_leaf.jpg.label: SoybeanLeaf
0252_soybean_leaf.jpg.caption: The soybean leaf appears green with a generally oval shape and serrated edges.
0252_soybean_leaf.jpg.management: Soybeans require warm weather (20-30°C / 68-86°F), full sun, and well-drained soil with pH 6.0-7.0. Plant after frost danger when soil temperature reaches 15°C (60°F). Inoculate seed with proper Bradyrhizobium strain if soybeans haven't been grown in field before. Soybeans fix atmospheric nitrogen so require little nitrogen fertilizer. Ensure adequate phosphorus and potassium. Maintain moisture especially during pod fill. Control weeds early in season. Monitor for soybean aphids, Japanese beetles, and stink bugs. Practice crop rotation. Harvest when leaves drop and pods rattle.

0255_squash_leaf.jpg.label: SquashLeaf
0255_squash_leaf.jpg.caption: Squash leaves that are affected by a disease may display yellowing, wilting, and dark spots or lesions.
0255_squash_leaf.jpg.management: Squash requires warm weather (18-30°C / 65-85°F), full sun, and fertile, well-drained soil with pH 6.0-6.8. Plant after frost danger when soil is warm. Provide hills or mounds for good drainage. Ensure consistent moisture (2.5-5 cm per week) especially during fruit development. Mulch to conserve moisture and suppress weeds. Apply balanced fertilization at planting and side-dress when vines begin running. Train vining types on trellises if desired. Monitor for squash vine borers, squash bugs, and cucumber beetles. Practice crop rotation. Harvest regularly to encourage continued production.

0258_strawberry_leaf.jpg.label: StrawberryLeaf
0258_strawberry_leaf.jpg.caption: The strawberry leaf appears green with serrated edges and a sprawling shape.
0258_strawberry_leaf.jpg.management: Strawberries require full sun, well-drained soil with pH 5.5-6.5, and consistent moisture. Plant in raised beds in heavy soils. Use plastic mulch or straw mulch to keep fruit clean and reduce disease. Renovate June-bearing types after harvest by mowing and narrowing rows. Remove old leaves and thin plants. Apply balanced fertilization after renovation and in early spring. Ensure adequate but not excessive nitrogen. Maintain consistent moisture through drip irrigation. Monitor for spider mites, tarnished plant bugs, and diseases. Remove daughter plants beyond desired row width. Replace plantings every 3-4 years.

0261_tobacco_leaf.jpg.label: TobaccoLeaf
0261_tobacco_leaf.jpg.caption: The tobacco leaf has a broad, ovate shape with a smooth texture and prominent veins.
0261_tobacco_leaf.jpg.management: Tobacco requires warm weather (20-30°C / 68-86°F), full sun, and well-drained, fertile soil with pH 5.5-6.5. Start from transplants. Ensure consistent moisture especially during leaf development. Apply balanced fertilization with adequate nitrogen and potassium - fertility requirements vary by tobacco type. Top plants (remove flower heads) to direct growth to leaves. Sucker (remove axillary shoots) regularly for some types. Monitor for tobacco hornworm, aphids, and flea beetles. Watch for viral diseases and bacterial infections. Cure leaves properly after harvest according to tobacco type (air-cured, flue-cured, or fire-cured).

0264_tomato_leaf.jpg.label: TomatoLeaf
0264_tomato_leaf.jpg.caption: Tomato leaf may appear yellow or wilted, with brown or black spots or lesions, and curling or twisting.
0264_tomato_leaf.jpg.management: Tomatoes require warm weather (18-29°C / 65-85°F), full sun, and well-drained soil with pH 6.0-6.8. Stake or cage plants for support and better air circulation. Apply mulch to conserve moisture and prevent soil-borne diseases. Water consistently at base of plants (2.5-5 cm per week). Apply balanced fertilization at planting and side-dress during fruit development. Remove suckers on indeterminate varieties. Monitor for hornworms, whiteflies, and aphids. Practice crop rotation. Harvest when fruit is fully colored and slightly soft. Proper calcium prevents blossom end rot.
